My expertise covers consequential damages clauses (including exclusion and carve-out drafting), SLA breach thresholds and remedy escalation frameworks, and cross-jurisdiction indemnity analysis addressing conflicts of law, cap enforceability, and varying standards for indirect loss recovery across common-law and civil-law regimes.

concept:material_breach
concept:material_breach
concept:material_breach

utterance.text: A material breach under contract law occurs when a party fails to perform a substantial obligation that goes to the essence of the agreement, depriving the non-breaching party of the benefit it reasonably expected—applied to SLA clause 14.2, this means a failure to maintain the guaranteed uptime threshold (typically 99.9%) constitutes a material breach because continuous service availability is a fundamental obligation upon which the customer's reliance and the contract's core value depend. The non-breaching party would, upon such failure and any applicable cure-period expiration, be entitled to invoke termination rights and seek remedies (including service credits or damages) as specified under clause 14.2 and the agreement's general breach provisions.

context.topic: concept:material_breach

utterance.text: A material breach under contract law occurs when a party fails to perform a substantial obligation that goes to the essence of the agreement, depriving the non-breaching party of the benefit it reasonably expected—applied to SLA clause 14.2, this means a failure to maintain the guaranteed uptime threshold (typically 99.9%) constitutes a material breach because continuous service availability is a fundamental obligation upon which the customer's reliance and the contract's core value depend. The non-breaching party would, upon such failure and any applicable cure-period expiration, be entitled to invoke termination rights and seek remedies (including service credits or damages) as specified under clause 14.2 and the agreement's general breach provisions.

context.topic: concept:substantial_performance

utterance.text: I believe that under the material breach analysis, the obligor's delivery of 92% of contracted services by the Day 60 milestone satisfies the substantial performance doctrine, meaning the non-breaching party cannot terminate but is limited to damages for the 8% shortfall—essentially, if the performance timeline shows near-complete delivery within the cure period, no material breach has occurred. I'm confident this framework applies because the timeline demonstrates the promisor acted in good faith toward completion, which should preclude the aggrieved party from invoking termination rights or consequential damages caps.
